message.content:
Here’s a straightforward way to structure it so you’re clear, honest, and not cruel.

You can think of it in parts:

---

### 1. Set the frame (why you’re talking)

- “I want to talk about us, and it’s not an easy conversation.”
- “This is uncomfortable for me, but I think we owe it to each other to be honest.”

This signals seriousness without drama.

---

### 2. Own that this is *your* perspective

- “I’ve been thinking a lot about how I’ve been feeling in our relationship.”
- “From my side, I’ve been feeling [lonely / disconnected / stressed / uncertain] for a while.”

Avoid “you always / you never” here. Keep it anchored in your experience.

---

### 3. Name the core issue, simply and directly

Pick language that matches where you actually are:

If you’re leaning toward ending it:
- “I don’t think this relationship is working for me anymore.”
- “I’ve reached a point where I think it might be better for us to end this relationship.”

If you’re still genuinely unsure:
- “I’m at a point where I’m not sure this relationship is right for me anymore.”
- “I’m questioning whether we should stay together.”

Try to use one clear sentence that doesn’t hide what you mean.

---

### 4. Briefly explain *why*, without attacking

Focus on patterns, not character:

- “I notice that we keep having the same conflicts about [x], and I don’t see us being able to resolve them.”
- “We want really different things around [kids / marriage / lifestyle / priorities], and I don’t see that changing.”
- “I don’t feel [supported / close / attracted / like myself] in this relationship in the way I need to.”

Two or three reasons are enough. You don’t have to build a legal case.

---

### 5. Be clear about what you’re asking for

If you’ve decided:
- “So I think the kindest thing, even though it hurts, is to end our relationship.”
- “I’m not saying this lightly, but I think we should break up.”

If you want a last attempt / break:
- “I don’t want to keep going as we have been. I think our options are either to [take a real break / separate / go to counseling / make specific changes] or to end things.”

Don’t leave it at “I’m unhappy” without saying what that implies.

---

### 6. Acknowledge that they matter to you

Without giving mixed signals:

- “You’re really important to me, and this is painful to say.”
- “I care about you and I appreciate [x, y], and that’s part of what makes this so hard.”

Avoid: “You’re amazing, you’ll find someone better” or “Maybe one day who knows…” if you’re actually ending it. That just muddies it.

---

### 7. Make space for their reaction

- “I know this is a lot. I want to hear what you’re feeling too.”
- “You don’t have to respond perfectly right now. We can pause if you need to.”

Be ready for silence, anger, or upset. You don’t have to fix their feelings, just not escalate.

---

### 8. Handle logistics / boundaries simply

Not in excruciating detail during the first blow, but at least signal it:

- “We can talk about practical things like [living situation / stuff / shared accounts] when you’re ready. It doesn’t have to be all figured out tonight.”
- “For the next few days, I think it’s best if we [don’t text / keep things minimal] so we both have some space.”

---

### Example you can adapt

You can edit this to sound like you:

> “I want to talk about us, and it’s not easy for me to say this.  
> I’ve been thinking a lot about how I’ve been feeling in our relationship, and from my side I’ve been feeling really [disconnected / unhappy] for a while.  
> I notice we keep running into the same issues around [x and y], and I don’t see that really changing, even though we’ve tried.  
> Because of that, I’ve come to the conclusion that this relationship isn’t working for me anymore, and I think we should end it. I don’t say that lightly. You’re important to me, and that’s part of what makes this so hard.  
> I know this might be a shock and you might be angry or upset, and that’s understandable. I do want to hear what you’re feeling. We don’t have to figure out every detail tonight, but we can work out the practical stuff when we’ve both
